title: Matthew 17
subtitle: The Transfiguration; Elijah Has Come; Failed Exorcism; Second Passion Prediction; Temple Tax

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — The Transfiguration; Elijah Has Already Come

heb.paragraph: Jesus was 'transfigured' — metamorphoō means a change of form revealing inner reality. The transfiguration does not add glory to Jesus; it temporarily reveals the glory always present but normally hidden. For one moment, the disciples see what is always true.

Peter offers to build three 'tents' (skēnai), echoing the Feast of Tabernacles and perhaps the wilderness tabernacle. He wants to institutionalise the moment. The Father interrupts: 'Listen to him.' The point is not staying on the mountain but hearing the Son and following him down.

panels.ctx: The transfiguration (vv.1–9) is the visual confirmation of Peter's confession (16:16) and the counterweight to the passion announcement (16:21): the one who must suffer is the one whose face shines like the sun. Moses and Elijah represent Torah and the Prophets — the whole of the OT — in conversation with Jesus, whose presence supersedes them (v.8: "they saw no one except Jesus"). The cloud and voice repeat the baptism's theophany with the crucial addition: "Listen to him." The question about Elijah (vv.10–13) then confirms that the Elijah of Malachi 4 was John the Baptist — and that John's fate was the pattern for the Son of Man's.

tl.name: Six days later — Transfiguration — this passage
tl.text: "After six days Jesus took Peter, James and John with him." Six days after the confession at Caesarea Philippi. The same three disciples who will witness the agony of Gethsemane are first shown the glory of the Transfiguration. The sequence is deliberate: glory before suffering.

poi.name: High mountain (Transfiguration)
poi.coords: Traditionally Mount Tabor or Mount Hermon
poi.text: Six days after Caesarea Philippi, Jesus takes Peter, James, and John up a high mountain. The traditional identification is Mount Tabor (Jezreel Valley) or Mount Hermon (near Caesarea Philippi, which is more geographically consistent with "six days later"). The mountain is the consistent space of theophany.

cross.note: The cloud of divine presence on Sinai; Moses's face shining. The transfiguration echoes both: the glory-cloud of God's presence; the shining face of the one who stands in God's presence. Jesus is the new Moses — and more.

I will send you the prophet Elijah before the great and dreadful day of the LORD. Jesus identifies John as this Elijah — and immediately applies the pattern to himself: they did to him what they wished, and so with the Son of Man.

notes.note: His face shone like the sun. The superlative brightness in the NT: brighter than Sinai, brighter than any comparison the disciples had seen. This is the glory of the pre-existent Son becoming visible — the Shekinah-glory that the temple was meant to house, now resident in a person. The disciples fall on their faces (v.6) in the appropriate theophanic response.

Listen to him. The three-word command added to the baptismal declaration. In the immediate context of ch.16's passion announcement and Peter's resistance, "listen to him" means: hear what he said about the cross and believe it, even though it violates your expectations. The voice from the cloud is answering Peter's "this shall never happen to you" with divine authority.

Elijah has already come, and they did not recognise him. The pattern of rejection is now traceable: John (Elijah) → killed; the Son of Man → to suffer in the same way. The transfiguration's glory and the passion's suffering are not contradictions but the two dimensions of the same identity.

Calvin on the Transfiguration: 'Christ was transfigured not to display his glory permanently during the earthly ministry but to give his disciples a momentary glimpse so that they might endure the scandal of the cross. The glory was real but brief -- a down-payment, not the final possession. Faith must hold the glimpsed glory through the long darkness that follows.'

Calvin on John as Elijah: 'John was not literally Elijah, but he came in the spirit and power of Elijah (Luke 1:17). This fulfils the prophecy without requiring a literal reincarnation. The fulfilment is real and substantial; the literalism that demands the exact person misunderstands the nature of prophetic typology.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the cloud (nephele) in v.5 traces the Shekinah cloud of divine presence through the OT: the cloud at Sinai (Exod 24:15-18), the cloud in the wilderness tabernacle (Exod 40:34-38), the cloud filling Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11). The cloud at the Transfiguration places Jesus within the canonical sequence of divine-presence manifestations.

The NET note on the Elijah-who-must-come-first (Mal 4:5) discusses the two-Elijah pattern in Jewish expectation: a literal return of Elijah versus an Elijah-like figure. Jesus resolves the ambiguity by identifying John the Baptist as the Elijah who has already come -- and been rejected. The note traces the implications for understanding the Messiah's rejection that follows.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson on metamorphothe (he was transfigured, v.2): the same word as Rom 12:2 and 2 Cor 3:18 for spiritual transformation. The physical shining of Jesus's face and clothes is an uncloaking of the divine glory that the Incarnation veiled. Robertson notes that Moses and Elijah represent the Law and the Prophets bearing witness to Jesus -- the very authorities the Pharisees claim to follow.

Robertson traces the Elijah identification (v.13: 'then the disciples understood that he was speaking to them about John the Baptist'). The disciples finally grasp what Jesus has been implying: Malachi's Elijah who must come first (Mal 4:5) is John the Baptist, whose ministry of preparation has been completed -- and rejected -- before Jesus's own ministry.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The Catena records the patristic reading of the Transfiguration as the preview of the resurrection glory. Chrysostom: 'Before the cross, Christ shows the resurrection, so that the disciples may know that the suffering is not defeat but the path to the glory.' The three witnesses (Peter, James, John) are the same three who will witness the agony in Gethsemane -- glory and suffering have the same witnesses.

The Catena notes Origen's reading of the 'until the Son of Man is raised from the dead' instruction (v.9): the Transfiguration vision is sealed until the resurrection interprets it. The resurrection is the hermeneutical key to the Transfiguration. The disciples' confusion (v.10: but why do the teachers of the law say Elijah must come first?) shows they still cannot connect the suffering to the glory.

sections.header: Verses 14–23 — The Epileptic Boy; Second Passion Prediction

heb.paragraph: The disciples could not cast out the demon because of their 'little faith' (oligopistia). Faith the size of a mustard seed can move mountains — the issue is not quantity but quality. Even tiny faith in a great God accomplishes what great effort without faith cannot.

panels.ctx: The descent from the transfiguration into the failed exorcism (vv.14–20) is a deliberate contrast. The disciples who were not on the mountain have failed; faith the size of a mustard seed would have been enough, but their faith was insufficient. The second passion prediction (vv.22–23) follows — the disciples grieve, but do not yet understand. The temple tax episode (vv.24–27) closes the chapter on a surprising note: Jesus the Son, exempt from the Father's house-tax, pays it anyway to avoid causing offense. Sovereignty and servanthood coexist.

tl.name: Six days later — Transfiguration — this passage
tl.text: "After six days Jesus took Peter, James and John with him." Six days after the confession at Caesarea Philippi. The same three disciples who will witness the agony of Gethsemane are first shown the glory of the Transfiguration. The sequence is deliberate: glory before suffering.

poi.name: High mountain (Transfiguration)
poi.coords: Traditionally Mount Tabor or Mount Hermon
poi.text: Six days after Caesarea Philippi, Jesus takes Peter, James, and John up a high mountain. The traditional identification is Mount Tabor (Jezreel Valley) or Mount Hermon (near Caesarea Philippi, which is more geographically consistent with "six days later"). The mountain is the consistent space of theophany.

cross.note: The two-drachma temple tax — half a shekel per adult male Israelite, collected annually for the upkeep of the sanctuary. Jesus's exemption claim is based on the Son's relationship to the Father: he would not be taxed for his own Father's house.
Mustard seed faith — the parable used positively (kingdom growth) and now diagnostically (faith sufficient for the impossible). The same image carries both the promise and the rebuke.

notes.note: You unbelieving and perverse generation. The rebuke is addressed to the crowd and the disciples — but then privately the disciples ask Jesus to diagnose them specifically (v.19). Jesus does: little faith. The sharpness of v.17 is the sharpness of the teacher who knows what is possible and sees what is being missed.

Mustard seed faith. The smallest possible measure of genuine faith is sufficient for the impossible. The disciples' failure was not that their faith was small but that it was insufficient — perhaps performative, perhaps dependent on method rather than trust. Even a tiny amount of genuine, living faith is enough. The mountain-moving promise is not hyperbole but the description of what genuine trust in the living God produces.

So that we may not cause offense. The temple tax episode shows Jesus balancing his identity (Son, therefore exempt) with his mission (servant, therefore pays). The coin from the fish is not a parlour trick but a sign: the Son for whom the whole creation is ordered provides for his community's obligations through creation's resources.

Calvin on the disciples' failure: 'Their faith was genuine but its roots were not yet deep enough to produce the particular fruit of this particular demand. A faith that can move mountains is not a quantity of faith but a quality -- the faith that knows it relies entirely on God and acts accordingly. The disciples were relying partly on their commission and partly on their faith; neither alone is sufficient.'

Calvin on the temple tax: 'Christ pays not because he owes the tax but to avoid giving unnecessary offence. This teaches the principle of Christian liberty in indifferent matters: where a practice is not sinful and its refusal would cause stumbling, the Christian should comply -- not from obligation but from love. Principle is not surrendered; offence is avoided.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'faith as small as a mustard seed' discusses the apparent contradiction with v.17's 'little faith' diagnosis. The note explains: the disciples had some faith (v.20a) but not the particular quality of faith -- the wholehearted, undivided dependence -- required for this exorcism. The mustard seed is about quality (pure, undivided), not quantity.

The NET note on the coin in the fish's mouth (stater, v.27) discusses the practicalities: a stater was worth four drachmas, exactly enough for two temple taxes (one for Jesus, one for Peter). The note observes that Jesus did not work miracles for his own convenience but here works one specifically to honour an obligation while teaching the deeper principle of kingdom sonship.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson on the disciples' failure to exorcise the epileptic boy: Jesus attributes it to their oligopistia (little faith, v.20 -- the abstract noun form of oligopistos). Robertson notes the contrast: the disciples who have already been given authority to cast out demons (10:1) fail here. The failure is not of the authority conferred but of the faith exercised.

Robertson on the temple tax (didrachmon, the two-drachma coin, v.24): the annual half-shekel temple tax of Exod 30:13, collected from every adult Jewish male. Robertson traces Jesus's argument: sons of the king don't pay the king's taxes -- but to avoid scandal, he provides the tax anyway. The miracle of the coin in the fish's mouth is understated; Robertson notes Jesus simply says 'give it to them for me and you.'

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The Catena records Chrysostom's treatment of the disciples' failure: they had grown overconfident after the Transfiguration, relying on their authority rather than their dependence on God. Chrysostom: 'They had begun to trust in themselves; Christ corrects them by allowing the failure. The lesson is that miracle-working requires not merely the authority conferred but the faith that remains consciously dependent on the one who conferred it.'

The Catena notes that the second passion prediction (v.22-23) produces grief in the disciples -- they understand more than before, even if not fully. Chrysostom: 'They were going to be exceedingly grieved -- this is the mark of their love for him. They cannot yet understand resurrection, but they understand death. The grief is the fruit of attachment.'

sections.header: Verses 24–27 — The Temple Tax: A Fish with a Coin

heb.paragraph: The temple tax of two drachmas (half-shekel) funded temple operations. Jesus pays it to avoid 'offence' (skandalizō) but first establishes that as the King's son, he is exempt. He voluntarily submits to an obligation he could legitimately refuse — a pattern repeated at the cross.

ppl.name: Moses and Elijah
ppl.text: Appear with Jesus — the whole OT testifies to him. Then only Jesus remains.

ppl.name: Peter
ppl.text: Misunderstands the transfiguration; accurately answers the tax question. The learning curve continues.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased. Listen to him!"NoteThe baptismal declaration (3:17) repeated with the addition "listen to him." In the context of Peter's resistance to the passion (16:22) and the disciples' little faith, the divine command to hear Jesus is the answer to every form of theological resistance.

src.title: Mount Hermon
src.quote: The most probable transfiguration site — a 2,800m peak north of Caesarea Philippi. The six days connect to Exod 24:16 (the cloud covered Sinai for six days before God spoke on the seventh).
src.note: The Sinai typology is reinforced by the timing.

src.title: The temple tax
src.quote: Two drachmas = half a shekel. Collected by Jewish communities in the diaspora as well as in Palestine. Josephus records disputes about its collection. The Qumran community paid it once in a lifetime rather than annually.
src.note: The tax was a significant communal institution whose obligation Jesus acknowledges while claiming exemption.

rec.text: The Transfiguration (August 6) is one of the twelve Great Feasts of the Eastern Orthodox calendar — the visible disclosure of Christ's divine nature, the goal of theosis (participation in God's nature).
17:20 has been the text for countless sermons on the sufficiency of small, genuine faith. The danger of over-literalism is real — the point is not quantity but quality and genuineness of trust.

rows.label: The Transfiguration: Glory Before the Cross
rows.text: His face shone like the sun, and his clothes became as white as the light. The uncloaking of divine glory is the preview of resurrection. The same three disciples who witness the glory will witness the agony in Gethsemane.

rows.label: The Transfiguration: Glory Before the Cross
rows.text: His face shone like the sun, and his clothes became as white as the light. The uncloaking of divine glory is the preview of resurrection. The same three disciples who witness the glory will witness the agony in Gethsemane.

rows.label: Failed Exorcism: Faith vs Confidence — 17:14-21
rows.text: The disciples fail not because they lack authority but because they lack the undivided, dependent faith that exercises authority properly. The mustard seed is about quality, not quantity.

tx.title: This kind only comes out by prayer (and fasting)
tx.content: Many manuscripts omit v.21. The shorter text (omitting v.21) is supported by the best manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus). The verse appears to have been added from Mark 9:29.
tx.note: Even if v.21 is secondary, the principle it articulates is consistent with Jesus's teaching on prayer throughout Matthew.

positions.name: Insufficient faith
positions.argument: The disciples had faith but not the quality of undivided, dependent faith that this exorcism required. The mustard seed saying is about the purity of faith, not its quantity.

positions.name: Lack of prayer and fasting (v.21)
positions.argument: The failure was a failure of spiritual preparation -- prayer and fasting are the disciplines that develop the faith capable of this exorcism.

themes.note: Matthew 17 moves from the Transfiguration's preview of glory to the disciples' failure at the foot of the mountain. The chapter establishes the pattern: glimpsed glory, returned to ordinary reality, and the ongoing demands of dependent faith. The disciples cannot yet hold what they have seen.
